question:
Sixty-five pregnant women at a high risk of pregnancy-induced hypertension participated in a randomized controlled trial comparing 100 mg of aspirin daily and a matching placebo during the third trimester of pregnancy (Schiff et al., 1989). The observed rates of hypertension are shown in the following table:

\begin{tabular}{lcll}
\hline & Aspirin treated & Placebo treated & Total \\
\hline Hypertension & 4 & 11 & 15 \\
No hypertension & 30 & 20 & 50 \\
Total & 34 & 31 & 65 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Do these data suggest that daily aspirin reduces the risk of hypertension in the last trimester of pregnancy?
answer: The proportions of women developing hypertension were 0.1176 $(4 / 34)$ in the aspirin group and $0.3548(11 / 31)$ in the placebo group. The difference is 0.24 with a wide $95 \% \mathrm{CI}$ from 0.04 to 0.44 . The Chi squared test with Yates' correction gives $X^2=3.89(\mathrm{P}=0.049)$. There is thus a suggestion that aspirin may reduce the risk of hypertension among pregnant women, but the wide CI points to considerable uncertainty about the magnitude of the effect.
